text: High load strength training (≥80% 1RM) improves running economy in middle- and long-distance runners with a small effect size.
population: middle- and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)

text: Combined strength training methods (two or more methods together) produce moderate improvements in running economy at speeds of 10.00 to 14.45 km/h.
population: middle- and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)

text: Plyometric training improves running economy only at speeds ≤12.00 km/h, with a small effect size.
population: middle- and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)

text: Submaximal load strength training (40-79% 1RM) does not significantly improve running economy compared to endurance-only training.
population: middle- and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)

text: Isometric strength training does not significantly improve running economy compared to endurance-only training.
population: middle- and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)

text: High load strength training produces greater running economy improvements at higher running speeds, with running speed acting as a significant moderator.
population: middle- and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)

text: Athletes with higher VO2max derive greater running economy benefits from high load strength training, with VO2max acting as a significant moderator.
population: middle- and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)

text: Strength training programs of 6 to 24 weeks duration with 1-4 sessions per week are effective for improving running economy when using high loads, plyometrics, or combined methods.
population: middle- and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)
